input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) is employed using SysML v1.7 for requirement, functional, logical, and product‑level modeling. Functional and logical architecture is described with architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML Interface Block Diagrams (IBDs). Behaviour modelling can be expressed with MontiArc Automaton or SysML state‑machine diagrams. Detailed simulation and behavioural execution are achieved with MathWorks Simulink, supplemented by additional simulation tools. Source‑level development is supported in C++ and Java. System abstraction is maintained through methodologies such as RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Deterministic Models And Data: The DT is built through a Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) workflow that employs multiple modeling notations and tools. Geometric structure is captured in SysML component diagrams and optionally 3‑D CAD integration. Functional and logical architectures are expressed with ADL‑style connectors such as MontiArc Automaton and SysML v1.7 state machines, enabling representation of component interactions and control flow. Parameters and behavior are simulated with MathWorks Simulink models, and where rapid prototyping or runtime execution is required, the models are emitted to general‑purpose languages (C++ or Java). Data sources include simulation results, model‑generated runtime streams, and external stakeholder data exchanges within the MBSE repository. Model relationships are defined hierarchically across abstraction levels – requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer – and linked through ADL/Bag modeling constructs. Data management is performed by the model repository that aggregates model artifacts from SysML, MontiArc, Simulink, and code, enabling traceability and synchronization across the DT ecosystem.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering of the Digital Twin follows a Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) workflow that uses SysML for requirement, functional, logical, and product layers, and employs architecture description languages such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc for component‑connector modelling. Behaviour is captured by state machines or MontiArc automata and further refined in simulation environments including MathWorks Simulink. The resulting models can be animated, validated, and generically code‑generated into C++ or Java, providing a repeatable twinning process and evolution path whereby model transformations, simulation validation, and code evolution are tracked across the design life‑cycle.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Standardization in the described Digital Twin system is anchored in the adoption of the SysML modeling language (currently at its 1.7 release) and cross‑compatibility with other architecture description languages such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc. While explicit certification or interoperability frameworks are not enumerated, the usage of these widely accepted engineering tools aligns with established industry guidelines for system modelling and architecture description.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.
